en.extract:
The inland dotterel or inland plover (Peltohyas australis) is an endemic bird of the arid Australian interior. It forms loose flocks in sparsely vegetated gibber plain and claypans in the day where it loafs in the shade and eats shoots of shrubs. It is most often encountered at night when it forages on roads for insects. The relative remoteness of its habitat means that it is not well studied. The most detailed observations of the species were made by the South African arid-zone ornithology specialist Gordon Maclean in the 1970s. Alternate English names include Australian plover, inland plover, desert plover and prairie plover.


Description

The inland dotterel is a medium-sized plover with a distinctive cryptic plumage. Males and females are similarly sized: 19–23 cm (7.5–9 in) in length, a wingspan of 43–47 cm (17–19 in) a weight of 80–90 g (2.8–3.2 oz), and a short bill 1.7 cm (0.67 in).
It is unlikely to be confused with any other species when found in its normal habitat. Its upperparts are a rich sandy buff, mottled with dark brown. The black band across the crown extending down through the eye is unique. The face, ear coverts and neck are white, as is the vent. A broad black Y-shaped band extends from the hindneck down the sides of the neck across the breast to the centre of the belly. Below the band the breast, flanks and belly are sandy buff. The legs are a pale buff colour, with the feet noticeably darker. The eye is dark brown. It has a short dark bill. Males and females have similar plumage. Maclean observed that the birds moulted into a paler, less bold non-breeding plumage. Immature birds lack the distinct black markings on the head, neck and breast of the adults. It calls infrequently, most often a short quiet quick or guttural kroot or krrr when taking flight. The precocial young have short dense downy feathers, pinkish-buff or cream on the upperparts with a heavy pattern of dark brown blotches. The underparts are off white. The bill and legs are pale yellow.


Taxonomy and systematics
The inland dotterel was first documented in 1840 after Captain Charles Sturt collected an immature bird on one of his expeditions to the Australian interior and sent it to John Gould. Gould initially named it Eudromias australis (from the Greek eu, good, and dromos, runner, and australis, of the southern continent).
The inland dotterel is one of over 60 shorebird species in the family Charadriidae, although it is rarely seen near water. Its taxonomic position continues to cause debate. The initial scientific name Eudromias australis supposed a generic relationship to the Eurasian dotterel (E. morinellus) that does not exist. Some modern authors place it in Charadrius, most closely related to the oriental plover (C. veredus) on mtDNA and protein allozyme evidence. Most authorities currently recognize the monotypic genera Peltohyas for the inland dotterel. Baker supported Peltohyas and placed the inland dotterel in a clade with other Australasian endemic genera (which also coincidentally consist a single species) the red-kneed dotterel (Erythrogons cinctus) and the wrybill (Anaryhnchus frontalis) of New Zealand. The Australian ornithologist Gregory M. Mathews proposed the subspecies C. australis whitlocki for birds in western Australia based on supposedly darker plumage, but the validity of this has been disputed.


Distribution

The inland dotterel occurs widely in the arid south-east and south-west of Australia. Its distribution corresponds to areas below the 100mm summer rainfall isohyet. It can be found in suitable habitat within this range in all the mainland states.


Habitat
Inland dotterels prefer sparsely vegetated habitat with low cover of 200–400 mm (7.9–15.7 in) saltbush, bluebush or samphire to provide food and shelter. It is most often seen in gibber plains, clay pans and gravel flats. It is believed to have benefited from land clearance for agriculture after European settlement. Movements are not well known – there appear to be seasonal movements south in the spring and north in the summer and there seems to be some movement beyond its normal range when excessive rains or severe drought makes habitat unsuitable. Vagrant birds have been observed as far as Sydney, NSW.


Behaviour and ecology
Typically in the day the birds associate in loose flocks of 10-20 birds, occasionally hundreds of birds. They are generally inactive in daytime, although they will forage on plants. At dusk the flock disperses and night sees most activity with individual birds hunting a variety of insect prey. It is during this activity that they are often encountered on outback roads. They tolerate high temperatures but will seek cover if the temperature exceeds 40 C (104 F). When approached they prefer to run away rather than fly.


Food
During the day the fleshy tips of desert shrubs are eaten. Inland dotterel have supraorbital salt glands, and it is thought these glands enable them to remove the salt content of the plants and hence use herbivory to source water. They have occasionally been observed drinking with large flocks gathering at stock tanks and clay-pans. At night the diet is insectivorous and spiders, grasshoppers, beetles, ants and earwigs have been recorded in gut contents.


Breeding

Inland dotterels form monogamous pairs when breeding, and both birds care for the young. May breed solitarily or in small colonies of up to six nests. Believed to breed at any time of the year if conditions are suitable, but generally breeding not well known, much data restricted to captive pairs.  The nest is a shallow depression in bare ground, formed by the birds or taking advantage of a suitable natural depression. A clutch of three eggs is laid. The eggs are oval medium brown coloured with an irregular pattern of dark brown blotches, 3.7 cm x 2.7 cm.


Threats
In the inland dotterel's sparse desert habitat there are few significant threats. Foxes (Vulpes vulpes) have been observed predating eggs and young. Black falcon (Falco subniger) and nankeen kestrel (Falco cenchroides) have been observed attacking adults.


Parasites
Two parasitic lice have been recorded on inland dotterel, Austromenopon sp. and Quadraceps neoaustralis.


Conservation
While there are no reliable estimates, it is believed the across its range the population is relatively large and stable. The conservation classification at the IUCN is least concern. It is not listed in any Australian Federal or State acts, but is listed as vulnerable on the Victorian governments advisory list of threatened vertebrate fauna.


References


External links

BirdLife species factsheet for Peltohyas australis
"Inland dotterel media". Internet Bird Collection.
Interactive range map of Peltohyas australis at IUCN Red List
Audio recordings of Inland dotterel on Xeno-canto.

The '''inland dotterel''' or '''inland plover''' ('''''Peltohyas australis''''') is an [[Endemism|endemic]] bird of the arid Australian interior. It forms loose flocks in sparsely vegetated [[Desert pavement|gibber plain]] and claypans in the day where it loafs in the shade and eats shoots of shrubs. It is most often encountered at night when it forages on roads for insects.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|title = A review of the biology of the Australian desert waders, Stiltia and Peltohyas|last = Maclean|first = G.L.|date = 1973|journal = Emu |doi = 10.1071/MU973061|volume=73|issue = 2|pages=61–70 | bibcode=1973EmuAO..73...61M }}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|title = A field study of the Australian Dotterel|last = Maclean|first = G.L.|date = 1976|journal = Emu |doi = 10.1071/MU9760207|volume=76|issue = 4|pages=207–215 | bibcode=1976EmuAO..76..207M }}</ref><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|url =http://www.publish.csiro.au/mu/pdf/mu9800039c |title = Nocturnal feeding of the Inland Dotterel Peltohyas australis|last = McNamara|first = J.A.|date = 1980|journal = Emu |doi = 10.1071/MU9800039c|volume=80| issue=1 |pages=39–40| bibcode=1980EmuAO..80...39M |url-access = subscription}}</ref> The relative remoteness of its habitat means that it is not well studied. The most detailed observations of the species were made by the South African arid-zone ornithology specialist Gordon Maclean in the 1970s.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> Alternate English names include Australian plover, inland plover, desert plover and prairie plover.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|title = Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds. Volume 2: Raptors to Lapwings|last1 = Marchant|first1 = S.|publisher = Oxford University Press|year = 1993|isbn = 0-19-553069-1|location = Melbourne|pages = 884–891|last2 = Higgins|first2 = P.J.}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Inland dotterel Peltohyas australis.JPG|thumb|Captive bird photographed at Healesville Sanctuary, Victoria, Australia]]
The inland dotterel is a medium-sized plover with a distinctive cryptic plumage. Males and females are similarly sized: {{convert|19|–|23|cm|in|round=0.5|abbr=on}} in length, a wingspan of {{convert|43|–|47|cm|in|abbr=on}} a weight of {{convert|80|-|90|g|oz|abbr=on}}, and a short bill {{convert|1.7|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":2" />

It is unlikely to be confused with any other species when found in its normal habitat.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title = Shorebirds: An identification guide to the waders of the world|url = https://archive.org/details/shorebirds00haym|url-access = limited|last1 = Hayman|first1 = P.|publisher = Helm|year = 1986|isbn = 0-7136-3509-6|location = London|pages = [https://archive.org/details/shorebirds00haym/page/n308 308]–309|last2 = Marchant|first2 = J.|last3 = Prater|first3 = T.}}</ref> Its upperparts are a rich sandy buff, mottled with dark brown. The black band across the crown extending down through the eye is unique. The face, ear coverts and neck are white, as is the vent. A broad black Y-shaped band extends from the hindneck down the sides of the neck across the breast to the centre of the belly. Below the band the breast, flanks and belly are sandy buff. The legs are a pale buff colour, with the feet noticeably darker. The eye is dark brown. It has a short dark bill. Males and females have similar plumage.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" /><ref name=":4">{{Cite book|title = Shorebirds in Australia|last = Lane|first = B.|publisher = Nelson|year = 1987|isbn = 0-17-006824-2|location = Melbourne|pages = 76–77}}</ref> Maclean observed that the birds moulted into a paler, less bold non-breeding plumage.<ref name=":1" /> Immature birds lack the distinct black markings on the head, neck and breast of the adults.<ref name=":1" /> It calls infrequently, most often a short quiet quick or guttural kroot or krrr when taking flight.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|title = On the East Murchison. Four months' collecting trip|last = Whitlock|first = F.L.|date = 1909|journal = Emu |doi = 10.1071/MU909181|volume=9|page=181 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/379679 }}</ref> The precocial young have short dense downy feathers, pinkish-buff or cream on the upperparts with a heavy pattern of dark brown blotches. The underparts are off white. The bill and legs are pale yellow.<ref name=":3" />

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The inland dotterel was first documented in 1840 after [[Charles Sturt|Captain Charles Sturt]] collected an immature bird on one of his expeditions to the Australian interior and sent it to [[John Gould]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title = On Some Avian Types, Principally Gould's, in the Collection of the Academy.|last = Meyer de Schauensee|first = R.|date = 1957|journal = Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia|issue = 109}}</ref> Gould initially named it ''Eudromias australis'' (from the Greek ''eu'', good, and ''dromos'', runner, and ''australis,'' of the southern continent).

The inland dotterel is one of over 60 shorebird species in the family [[Charadriidae]], although it is rarely seen near water. Its taxonomic position continues to cause debate. The initial scientific name ''Eudromias australis'' supposed a generic relationship to the [[Eurasian dotterel]] (''E. morinellus'') that does not exist. Some modern authors place it in ''Charadrius,'' most closely related to the [[oriental plover]] (''C. veredus'') on [[Mitochondrial DNA|mtDNA]] and protein allozyme evidence.<ref name=":7">{{Cite book|title = Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds|url = https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri|url-access = limited|last1 = Christidis|first1 = L.|publisher = CSIRO|year = 2008|isbn = 978-0-643-09602-8|location = Australia |pages = [https://archive.org/details/systematicstaxon00chri/page/n143 133]–135|last2 = Boles|first2 = W.E.}}</ref> Most authorities currently recognize the monotypic genera ''Peltohyas'' for the inland dotterel.<ref name=":7" /><ref>{{Cite journal|title = Biochemical systematics of the Australian Dotterels and Plovers (Charadriiformes: Charadriidae)|last1 = Christian|first1 = P.D.|date = 1992|journal = Australian Journal of Zoology|doi = 10.1071/ZO9920225|last2 = Christidis|first2 = L.|issue = 2|last3 = Schodde|first3 = R. |volume=40 |page=225}}</ref> Baker supported ''Peltohyas'' and placed the inland dotterel in a clade with other Australasian endemic genera (which also coincidentally consist a single species) the [[red-kneed dotterel]] (''Erythrogons cinctus'') and the [[wrybill]] (''Anaryhnchus frontalis'') of New Zealand. The Australian ornithologist [[Gregory Mathews|Gregory M. Mathews]] proposed the subspecies ''C. australis whitlocki'' for birds in western Australia based on supposedly darker plumage, but the validity of this has been disputed.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal|url = https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/3109567|title = A reference list to the birds of Australia|last = Mathews|first = G.M. |date = 1912|journal = Novitates Zoologicae|doi = 10.5962/bhl.part.1694|volume=18|pages=171–446|doi-access = free}}</ref>

==Distribution==
[[File:Gibber plains SNP.JPG|thumb|Gibber plains, Sturt National Park]]
The inland dotterel occurs widely in the arid south-east and south-west of Australia. Its distribution corresponds to areas below the [http://www.bom.gov.au/jsp/ncc/climate_averages/rainfall/index.jsp?period=sum&area=oz#maps 100mm summer rainfall isohyet].<ref name=":2" /> It can be found in suitable habitat within this range in all the mainland states.<ref name=":2" />

==Habitat==
Inland dotterels prefer sparsely vegetated habitat with low cover of {{convert|200|–|400|mm|in|abbr=on}} [[Atriplex|saltbush]], bluebush or samphire to provide food and shelter.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" /> It is most often seen in gibber plains, clay pans and gravel flats. It is believed to have benefited from land clearance for agriculture after European settlement.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = A Note on the Australian Dotterel|last = Bryant|first = C.E.|date = 1939|journal = Emu |doi = 10.1071/MU939153|volume=39|issue = 3|pages=153–155}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title = Can Pastoral Grazing Management Satisfy Endorsed Conservation Objectives in Arid Western Australia?|last1 = Curry|first1 = P.J.|date = 1990|journal = Journal of Environmental Management|doi = 10.1016/0301-4797(90)90025-r|last2 = Hacker|first2 = R.B.|issue = 4 |volume=30 |pages=295–320 | bibcode=1990JEnvM..30..295C }}</ref> Movements are not well known – there appear to be seasonal movements south in the spring and north in the summer and there seems to be some movement beyond its normal range when excessive rains or severe drought makes habitat unsuitable.<ref name=":4" /> Vagrant birds have been observed as far as Sydney, NSW.<ref name=":3" />

== Behaviour and ecology ==
Typically in the day the birds associate in loose flocks of 10-20 birds, occasionally hundreds of birds.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":4" /><ref>{{Cite book|title = The Atlas of Australian birds|last1 = Blakers|first1 = M.|publisher = Melbourne University Press|year = 1984|isbn = 0-522-84285-2|location = Melbourne|last2 = Davies|first2 = S.J.J.F.|last3 = Reilly|first3 = P.N.}}</ref> They are generally inactive in daytime, although they will forage on plants.<ref name=":1" /> At dusk the flock disperses and night sees most activity with individual birds hunting a variety of insect prey.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> It is during this activity that they are often encountered on outback roads.<ref name=":4" /> They tolerate high temperatures but will seek cover if the temperature exceeds 40 C (104 F).<ref name=":1" /> When approached they prefer to run away rather than fly.<ref name=":1" />

===Food===
During the day the fleshy tips of desert shrubs are eaten. Inland dotterel have supraorbital [[salt gland]]s, and it is thought these glands enable them to remove the salt content of the plants and hence use herbivory to source water.<ref name=":1" /> They have occasionally been observed drinking with large flocks gathering at stock tanks and clay-pans.<ref>{{Cite journal|url = https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/9370515|title = The drinking habits of Peltohyas australis (Australian dotterel)|last = McGilp|first = J.N.|date = 1922|journal = South Australian Ornithologist|access-date = 16 October 2015}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title = A Trip in Central Australia in Midsummer|last = Cooper|first = R.P.|date = 1941|journal = Emu|doi = 10.1071/MU941101|issue = 2 |volume=41 |pages=101–111 | bibcode=1941EmuAO..41..101C }}</ref> At night the diet is insectivorous and [[spider]]s, [[grasshoppers]], [[beetle]]s, [[ant]]s and [[earwig]]s have been recorded in gut contents.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":5" />

===Breeding===
[[File:Emu volume 9 plate 16.jpg|thumb|Photo of nest site with eggs by [[F. Lawson Whitlock|Whitlock]], East Murchison, 1909.]]
Inland dotterels form monogamous pairs when breeding, and both birds care for the young.<ref name=":0" /> May breed solitarily or in small colonies of up to six nests. Believed to breed at any time of the year if conditions are suitable,<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|title = Notes on some South Australian waders|last = Boehm|first = E.F.|date = 1960|journal = Emu|doi = 10.1071/MU960211|issue = 3 |volume=60 |pages=211–218 | bibcode=1960EmuAO..60..211B }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title = Bird observations on the North-western Nullarbor Plain and neighbouring regions, 1967-1978|last1 = Brooker|first1 = M.G.|date = 1979|journal = Emu|doi = 10.1071/MU9790176|last2 = Ridpath|first2 = M.G.|issue = 4|last3 = Estbergs|first3 = J.A.|last4 = Bywater|first4 = J.|last5 = Hart|first5 = D.S.|last6 = Jones|first6 = M.S. |volume=79 |page=176 | bibcode=1979EmuAO..79..176B }}</ref> but generally breeding not well known, much data restricted to captive pairs.<ref name=":2" />  The nest is a shallow depression in bare ground, formed by the birds or taking advantage of a suitable natural depression.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":6" /><ref name=":8" /> A clutch of three eggs is laid. The eggs are oval medium brown coloured with an irregular pattern of dark brown blotches, 3.7 cm x 2.7 cm.<ref name=":2" />

=== Threats ===
In the inland dotterel's sparse desert habitat there are few significant threats. [[Fox]]es (''Vulpes vulpes'') have been observed predating eggs and young.<ref name=":2" /> [[Black falcon]] (''Falco subniger'') and [[nankeen kestrel]] (''Falco cenchroides'') have been observed attacking adults.<ref name=":1" />

=== Parasites ===
Two parasitic lice have been recorded on inland dotterel, ''Austromenopon'' sp. and ''[[Quadraceps]] neoaustralis.''<ref>{{Cite journal|url = http://www.phthiraptera.info/Publications/0402.pdf|title = Two new species of Quadraceps (Mallophaga: Philopteridae) from Australia|last1 = Emerson|first1 = K.C.|date = 1986|journal = Proceedings of the Entomological Society of Washington|last2 = Price|first2 = R.D.|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160304080109/http://www.phthiraptera.info/Publications/0402.pdf|archive-date = 2016-03-04}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title = Lice (Insecta: Phthiraptera) from some Australian birds|last1 = Stranger|first1 = R.H.|date = 1988|journal = Records of the Western Australian Museum|last2 = Palma|first2 = R.L.|issue = 19}}</ref>

==Conservation==
While there are no reliable estimates, it is believed the across its range the population is relatively large and stable. The conservation classification at the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature|IUCN]] is [[Least-concern species|least concern]]. It is not listed in any Australian Federal or State acts,{{citation needed|date=November 2017}} but is listed as vulnerable on the Victorian governments advisory list of threatened vertebrate fauna.<ref>{{cite book|author1=The State of Victoria Department of Sustainability and Environment|title=Advisory List of Threatened Vertebrate Fauna in Victoria|date=2013|publisher=Victorian Government Department of Sustainability and Environment|location=Melbourne|isbn=978-1-74287-504-0|chapter-url=https://www.environment.vic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0014/50450/Advisory-List-of-Threatened-Vertebrate-Fauna_FINAL-2013.pdf|archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20140203162740/http://www.depi.vic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/210439/Advisory-List-of-Threatened-Vertebrate-Fauna_FINAL-2013.pdf|archive-date = 2014-02-03|url-status=live|page=11|chapter=Birds}}</ref>

zh.extract:
澳洲小嘴鴴（学名：Peltohyas australis）是澳大利亚干旱内陆地区特有的一种鸟类。它白天常成稀疏的群体活动，栖息于植被稀少的岩漠与黏土洼地，在阴凉处休憩，并以灌木的新芽为食；夜间则常出现在道路上，捕食昆虫。由于其栖息地偏远，这一物种的研究仍相当有限。关于澳洲小嘴鴴的最详尽观察资料，来自南非干旱区鸟类学专家戈登·麦克林（Gordon Maclean）于1970年代的研究。
